d113b989-4eb8-429b-bc8f-56165c81de13.label: text_chunk
d113b989-4eb8-429b-bc8f-56165c81de13.text:
arXiv:1610.00479v3  [cs.CL]  1 May 2017
Nonsymbolic Text Representation 0 Hinrich Sch ¨utze, Heike Adel, Ehsaneddin Asgari CIS, LMU Munich, Germany inquiries@cislmu.org

Abstract

We introduce the first generic text repre- sentation model that is completely non- symbolic, i.e.,

c27ab66e-ca85-4935-9297-e8c3a881f369.label: text_chunk
c27ab66e-ca85-4935-9297-e8c3a881f369.text:
it does not require the avail- ability of a segmentation or tokenization method that attempts to identify words or other symbolic units in text. This applies to training the representations as well as to using them in an application. We demon- strate better performance than prior work on entity typing and text denoising.

1 Introduction

Character-level models can be grouped into three classes.

5718ce3e-9088-4b12-83f6-d33bb661b2cd.label: text_chunk
5718ce3e-9088-4b12-83f6-d33bb661b2cd.text:
(i) End-to-end models learn a separate model on the raw character (or byte) input for each task; these models estimate task-specific pa- rameters, but no representation of text that would be usable across tasks is computed.

 Throughout this paper, we refer to r ( x ) as the “representa- tion” of x only if r ( x ) is a generic rendering of x that can be used in a general way, e.g., across tasks and domains.

52cc6054-ede8-430c-ae4d-8ec5ce8a11fe.label: text_chunk
52cc6054-ede8-430c-ae4d-8ec5ce8a11fe.text:
The activation pattern of a hidden layer for a given input sentence in a mul- tilayer perceptron (MLP) is not a representation according to this definition if it is not used out- side of the MLP.

 (ii) Character-level models of words derive a representation of a word w from the character string of w , but they are symbolic in that they need text segmented into tokens as input.

47d31538-ae14-45f3-813f-8070ea903589.label: text_chunk
47d31538-ae14-45f3-813f-8070ea903589.text:
(iii) Bag-of-character-ngram models, bag- of-ngram models for short, use character ngrams to encode sequence-of-character information, but sequence-of-ngram information is lost in the rep- resentations they produce.Our premise is that text representations are needed in NLP.

 A large body of work on word
embeddings demonstrates that a generic text rep- resentation, trained in an unsupervised fashion on large corpora, is useful.

50029768-5b1d-4477-bbbf-754afff17d3a.label: text_chunk
50029768-5b1d-4477-bbbf-754afff17d3a.text:
Thus, we take the view that group (i) models , end-to-end learning with- out any representation learning, is not a good gen- eral approach for NLP.We distinguish training and utilization of the text representation model.

 We use “training” to refer to the method by which the model is learned and “utilization” to refer to the application of the model to a piece of text to compute a representa- tion of the text.

c5d0b8ca-f203-46fe-b883-36de8ebe7624.label: text_chunk
c5d0b8ca-f203-46fe-b883-36de8ebe7624.text:
In many text representation mod- els, utilization is trivial. For example, for word embedding models, utilization amounts to a sim- ple lookup of a word to get its precomputed em- bedding.

 However, for the models we consider, utilization is not trivial and we will discuss differ- ent approaches.Both training and utilization can be either sym- bolic or nonsymbolic .

8fdd9f10-7f76-419d-bd6b-87118ba29a47.label: text_chunk
8fdd9f10-7f76-419d-bd6b-87118ba29a47.text:
We define a symbolic ap- proach as one that is based on tokenization, i.e., a segmentation of the text into tokens. Symbol iden- tifiers (i.e.

, tokens) can have internal structure – a tokenizer may recognize tokens like “to and fro” and “London-based” that contain delimiters – and may be morphologically analyzed downstream.

315ae6ff-ea9d-4944-9639-21288f17d6aa.label: text_chunk
315ae6ff-ea9d-4944-9639-21288f17d6aa.text:
1
We define a nonsymbolic approach as one that is tokenization-free, i.e., no assumption is made that there are segmentation boundaries and that
0 A short version of this paper appears as (Sch¨utze, 2017).

1 Two comments on how we use the term “symbolic”. (i) Some define symbols as identifiers that have no internal structure, but uses of the term that allow for the possibil- ity of internal structure are also frequent.

ddc64e8a-b96e-448a-ad82-0fb3c410a3d2.label: text_chunk
ddc64e8a-b96e-448a-ad82-0fb3c410a3d2.text:
For example, the Wikipedia entry for “symbol” states: “Numerals are symbols for numbers.” Most numerals have internal structure. (ii) The position-embedding representation of a text introduced below is a sequence of position embeddings.

 An embedding that represents a single character must be viewed as symbolic since a character is a symbol – just like a representation of text as a sequence of word embeddings is symbolic since each word corresponds to a symbol. But position embeddings do not represent single characters.

9f4cc3a6-f5ee-48a9-8e61-5b84911ef457.label: text_chunk
9f4cc3a6-f5ee-48a9-8e61-5b84911ef457.text:
See § 4.each segment (e.g., a word) should be represented (e.g., by a word embedding) in a way that is in- dependent of the representations (e.g.

, word em- beddings) of neighboring segments.

c704564f-315f-4801-a77c-aeb220575c15.label: text_chunk
c704564f-315f-4801-a77c-aeb220575c15.text: Methods for training text representation models that require to- kenized text include word embedding models like word2vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) and most group (ii) methods , i.e., character-level models like fast- Text skipgram (Bojanowski et al., 2017).

972d6c61-2511-427e-967c-e76c49b55acf.label: text_chunk
972d6c61-2511-427e-967c-e76c49b55acf.text: Bag-of-ngram models , group (iii) models, are text representation utilization models that typ- ically compute the representation of a text as the sum of the embeddings of all character ngrams occurring in it, e.g., WordSpace (Sch¨utze, 1992) and CHARAGRAM (Wieting et al., 2016).

3257dcd3-e9c3-45da-b119-f13cd5ce3910.label: text_chunk
3257dcd3-e9c3-45da-b119-f13cd5ce3910.text: WordSpace and CHARAGRAM are examples of mixed training-utilization models: training is per- formed on tokenized text (words and phrases), 2 utilization is nonsymbolic.We make two contributions in this paper. (i) We propose the first generic method for training text representation models without the need for tok- enization and address the challenging sparseness issues that make this difficult.

5e735096-f303-4901-a3d4-621de3891264.label: text_chunk
5e735096-f303-4901-a3d4-621de3891264.text:
(ii) We propose the first nonsymbolic utilization method that fully rep- resents sequence information – in contrast to uti- lization methods like bag-of-ngrams that discard sequence information that is not directly encoded in the character ngrams themselves.

2 Motivation

Chung et al. (2016b) give two motivations for their work on character-level models.

bcf87d7f-46c4-4475-9246-6130880c0b2a.label: text_chunk
bcf87d7f-46c4-4475-9246-6130880c0b2a.text:
First, tokeniza- tion (or, equivalently, segmentation) algorithms make many mistakes and are brittle: “we do not have a perfect word segmentation algorithm for any one language”. Tokenization errors then prop- agate throughout the NLP pipeline.Second, there is currently no general solu- tion for morphology in statistical NLP.

 For many languages, high-coverage and high-quality mor- phological resources are not available.

7168818a-86a2-4fc5-a217-7fb2990751c6.label: text_chunk
7168818a-86a2-4fc5-a217-7fb2990751c6.text:
Even for well resourced languages, problems like ambigu- ity make morphological processing difficult; e.g., “rung” is either the singular of a noun meaning “part of a ladder” or the past participle of “to ring”. In many languages, e.g., in German, syncretism, a particular type of systematic morphological ambi-


guity, is pervasive.

48656f18-4d8a-4a11-a9de-0d7abd75c4ea.label: text_chunk
48656f18-4d8a-4a11-a9de-0d7abd75c4ea.text: Thus, there is no simple mor- phological processing method that would produce a representation in which all inflected forms of “to ring” are marked as having a common lemma; and no such method in which an unseen form like “aromatizing” is reliably analyzed as a form of “aromatize” whereas an unseen form like “anti- trafficking” is reliably analyzed as the compound “anti+trafficking”.

18e78f4d-8328-4fe6-bfea-0061225e899b.label: text_chunk
18e78f4d-8328-4fe6-bfea-0061225e899b.text: Of course, it is an open question whether non- symbolic methods can perform better than mor- phological analysis, but the foregoing discussion motivates us to investigate them.Chung et al. (2016b) focus on problems with the tokens produced by segmentation algorithms. Equally important is the problem that tokeniza- tion fails to capture structure across multiple tokens .

b35170f4-0e49-4c4e-9486-a0adb84d61d7.label: text_chunk
b35170f4-0e49-4c4e-9486-a0adb84d61d7.text:
The job of dealing with cross-token struc- ture is often given to downstream components of the pipeline, e.g.

, components that recognize mul- tiwords and named entitites in English or in fact any word in a language like Chinese that uses no overt delimiters.

05339426-384f-42d8-8314-636ddb4c4e88.label: text_chunk
05339426-384f-42d8-8314-636ddb4c4e88.text:
However, there is no linguistic or computational reason in principle why we should treat the recognition of a unit like “electromechan- ical” (containing no space) as fundamentally dif- ferent from the recognition of a unit like “electrical engineering” (containing a space).

 Character-level models offer the potential of uniform treatment of such linguistic units.

05fa339f-2966-4d38-9b92-24ae5d5f4d28.label: text_chunk
05fa339f-2966-4d38-9b92-24ae5d5f4d28.text:
3 Text representation model: Training 3.1 Methodology

Many text representation learning algorithms can be understood as estimating the parameters of the model from a unit-context matrix C where each row corresponds to a unit u i , each column to a context c j and each cell C ij measures the degree of association between u i and c j .

ddfd0a0c-6af1-4c7e-897a-6cfbf731eb44.label: text_chunk
ddfd0a0c-6af1-4c7e-897a-6cfbf731eb44.text: For example, the skipgram model is closely related to an SVD fac- torization of a pointwise mutual information ma- trix (Levy and Goldberg, 2014); in this case, both units and contexts are words. Many text represen- tation learning algorithms are formalized as ma- trix factorization (e.g., (Deerwester et al., 1990; Hofmann, 1999; Stratos et al.

39ad3f9b-ebc1-4753-a4ed-f411f3d23128.label: text_chunk
39ad3f9b-ebc1-4753-a4ed-f411f3d23128.text: , 2015)), but there may be no big difference between implicit (e.g., (Pennington et al., 2014)) and explicit factoriza- tion methods; see also (Mohamed, 2011; Rastogi et al., 2015).Our goal in this paper is not to develop new ma-

bab40962-1582-4acd-82a6-2d1639cf043d.label: text_chunk
bab40962-1582-4acd-82a6-2d1639cf043d.text: 2 The WordSpace model could be trained on text that is not tokenized, but the redundancy of overlapping character ngrams could be a problem: see discussion below.trix factorization methods. Instead, we will focus on defining the unit-context matrix in such a way that no symbolic assumption has to be made. This unit-context matrix can then be processed by any existing or still to be invented algorithm.Definition of units and contexts.

9c484f7b-e378-48cd-a340-d09aa9f8e091.label: text_chunk
9c484f7b-e378-48cd-a340-d09aa9f8e091.text: How to de- fine units and contexts without relying on segmen- tation boundaries? In initial experiments, we sim- ply generated all character ngrams of length up to k max (where k max is a parameter), including char- acter ngrams that cross token boundaries; i.e., no segmentation is needed.

363fe657-fe23-4ab5-9c0a-22efa22761c9.label: text_chunk
363fe657-fe23-4ab5-9c0a-22efa22761c9.text: We then used a skipgram- type objective for learning embeddings that at- tempts to predict, from ngram g 1 , an ngram g 2 in g 1 ’s context. Results were poor because many training instances consist of pairs ( g 1 , g 2 ) in which g 1 and g 2 overlap, e.g., one is a subsequence of the other.

c0b99402-4739-4b06-9a5e-2a7e793af850.label: text_chunk
c0b99402-4739-4b06-9a5e-2a7e793af850.text: So the objective encourages trivial pre- dictions of ngrams that have high string similarity with the input and nothing interesting is learned.In this paper, we propose an alternative way of defining units and contexts that supports well- performing nonsymbolic text representation learn- ing: multiple random segmentation . A pointer moves through the training corpus. The current position i of the pointer defines the left boundary of the next segment.

63c59a20-2976-49b5-a917-d4b71697c9e4.label: text_chunk
63c59a20-2976-49b5-a917-d4b71697c9e4.text: The length l of the next move is uniformly sampled from [ k min , k max ] where k min and k max are the minimum and maximum segment lengths. The right boundary of the segment is then i + l . Thus, the segment just generated is c i,i + l , the subsequence of the corpus between (and includ- ing) positions i and i + l .

900de01c-aea3-487b-beba-8c2b20271f01.label: text_chunk
900de01c-aea3-487b-beba-8c2b20271f01.text:
The pointer is positioned at i + l + 1 , the next segment is sampled and so on.

 An example of a random segmentation from our experiments is “@he@had@b egu n@to@show @his@cap acity@f” where space was replaced with “@” and the next segment starts with “or@”.The corpus is segmented this way m times (where m is a parameter) and the m random seg- mentations are concatenated.

6ea0f8c6-2547-40ca-a869-a40e097eac57.label: text_chunk
6ea0f8c6-2547-40ca-a869-a40e097eac57.text:
The unit-context matrix is derived from this concatenated corpus.Multiple random segmentation has two advan- tages.

 First, there is no redundancy since, in any given random segmentation, two ngrams do not overlap and are not subsequences of each other. Second, a single random segmentation would only cover a small part of the space of possible ngrams.

2ba45feb-35c1-4a97-b945-68e23586b9f7.label: text_chunk
2ba45feb-35c1-4a97-b945-68e23586b9f7.text:
For example, a random segmentation of “a rose is a rose is a rose” might be “[a ros][e is a ros][e is][a rose]”. This segmentation does not contain


the segment “rose” and this part of the corpus can then not be exploited to learn a good embedding for the fourgram “rose”.

07b3ae32-3ff8-42dc-ac21-d53c33c82edf.label: text_chunk
07b3ae32-3ff8-42dc-ac21-d53c33c82edf.text:
However, with multiple random segmentation, it is likely that this part of the corpus does give rise to the segment “rose” in one of the segmentations and can contribute infor- mation to learning a good embedding for “rose”.

We took the idea of random segmentation from work on biological sequences (Asgari and Mofrad, 2015; Asgari and Mofrad, 2016).

46ab5ba9-41af-4bc5-bd75-167b858e703e.label: text_chunk
46ab5ba9-41af-4bc5-bd75-167b858e703e.text:
Such sequences have no delimiters, so they are a good model if one believes that delimiter-based segmentation is problematic for text.

3.2 Ngram equivalence classes/Permutation

Form-meaning homomorphism premise. Non- symbolic representation learning does not prepro- cess the training corpus by means of tokenization and considers many ngrams that would be ignored in tokenized approaches because they span token boundaries.

c8a52f59-887c-4630-bba6-98b31a909b7d.label: text_chunk
c8a52f59-887c-4630-bba6-98b31a909b7d.text:
As a result, the number of ngrams that occur in a corpus is an order of magnitude larger for tokenization-free approaches than for tokenization-based approaches. See supplemen- tary for details.

We will see below that this sparseness impacts performance of nonsymbolic text representation negatively. We address sparseness by defining ngram equivalence classes.

aca10bae-c26e-4f40-b39e-a2be08d5fd2f.label: text_chunk
aca10bae-c26e-4f40-b39e-a2be08d5fd2f.text:
All ngrams in an equivalence class receive the same embedding.The relationship between form and meaning is mostly arbitrary, but there are substructures of the ngram space and the embedding space that are systematically related by homomorphism. In this paper, we will assume the following homomor- phism:


g 1 ∼ τ g 2 ⇔ ~v ( g 1 ) ∼ = ~v ( g 2 )
where g 1 ∼ τ g 2

4e189387-947a-488e-ae3d-4ee4fcb4f661.label: text_chunk
4e189387-947a-488e-ae3d-4ee4fcb4f661.text:
iff τ ( g 1 ) = τ ( g 2 ) for string transduction τ and ~v ( g 1 ) ∼ = ~v ( g 2 ) iff | ~v ( g 1 ) − ~v ( g 2 ) | 2 < ǫ .

As a simple example consider a transduction τ that deletes spaces at the beginning of ngrams, e.g., τ ( @Mercedes ) = τ ( Mercedes ) .

35bef8e9-a24b-4da2-8191-45bb5985c555.label: text_chunk
35bef8e9-a24b-4da2-8191-45bb5985c555.text: This is an example of a meaning-preserving τ since for, say, English, τ will not change meaning. We will pro- pose a procedure for learning τ below.We define ∼ = as “closeness” – not as identity – because of estimation noise when embeddings are learned.

5a704284-4ea8-462e-a0f3-b2c929dac551.label: text_chunk
5a704284-4ea8-462e-a0f3-b2c929dac551.text:
We assume that there are no true syn- onyms and therefore the direction g 1 ∼ τ g 2 ⇐
~v ( g 1 ) ∼ = ~v ( g 2 ) also holds. For example, “car” and “automobile” are considered synonyms, but we assume that their embeddings are different be- cause only “car” has the literary sense “chariot”.

8ac165a5-b6b3-495c-847b-6ed9d7a4d6b6.label: text_chunk
8ac165a5-b6b3-495c-847b-6ed9d7a4d6b6.text: If they were identical, then the homomorphism would not hold since “car” and “automobile” can- not be converted into each other by any plausible meaning-preserving τ .Learning procedure.

ad25b59b-31e1-4182-977a-197e65190d33.label: text_chunk
ad25b59b-31e1-4182-977a-197e65190d33.text: To learn τ , we define three templates that transform one ngram into an- other: (i) replace character a 1 with character a 2 , (ii) delete character a 1 if its immediate predeces- sor is character a 2 , (iii) delete character a 1 if its immediate successor is character a 2 . The learning procedure takes a set of ngrams and their embed- dings as input.

51981dbc-f039-4275-a9cb-96fb74729fe4.label: text_chunk
51981dbc-f039-4275-a9cb-96fb74729fe4.text: It then exhaustively searches for all pairs of ngrams, for all pairs of characters a 1 / a 2 , for each of the three templates. (This takes about 10 hours on a multicore server.) When two match- ing embeddings exist, we compute their cosine.

8bc1d7dd-16af-4010-b51d-2a0ea44746c6.label: text_chunk
8bc1d7dd-16af-4010-b51d-2a0ea44746c6.text:
For example, for the operation “delete space be- fore M”, an ngram pair from our embeddings that matches is “@Mercedes” / “Mercedes” and we compute its cosine.

 As the characteristic statistic of an operation we take the average of all cosines; e.g., for “delete space before M” the average co- sine is .7435.

8bbac55a-2501-44d2-b8dc-54f8543a4b3c.label: text_chunk
8bbac55a-2501-44d2-b8dc-54f8543a4b3c.text: We then rank operations according to average cosine and take the first N o as the defi- nition of τ where N o is a parameter. For characters that are replaced by each other (e.g.

fbe6a85d-2aa1-45cb-b68b-4fa920f48b2a.label: text_chunk
fbe6a85d-2aa1-45cb-b68b-4fa920f48b2a.text: , 1, 2, 3 in Ta- ble 1), we compute the equivalence class and then replace the learned operations with ones that re- place a character by the canonical member of its equivalence class (e.g., 2 → 1, 3 → 1).Permutation premise. Tokenization algo- rithms can be thought of as assigning a particular function or semantics to each character and mak- ing tokenization decisions accordingly; e.g.

86c49ab4-3d2e-43f3-9902-f0a1aaf85489.label: text_chunk
86c49ab4-3d2e-43f3-9902-f0a1aaf85489.text: , they may disallow that a semicolon, the character “;”, occurs inside a token. If we want to learn represen- tations from the data without imposing such hard constraints, then characters should not have any particular function or semantics. A consequence of this desideratum is that if any two characters are exchanged for each other, this should not af- fect the representations that are learned.

7711d9f7-adc5-4e58-9ed9-c19bc7df1b2a.label: text_chunk
7711d9f7-adc5-4e58-9ed9-c19bc7df1b2a.text:
For ex- ample, if we interchange space and “A” throughout a corpus, then this should have no effect on learn- ing: what was the representation of “NATO” be- fore, should now be the representation of “N TO”. We can also think of this type of permutation as
a sanity check: it ensures we do not inadvertantly make use of text preprocessing heuristics that are pervasive in NLP. 3

196170eb-f8a8-4ac4-9896-ce4e9ddcfd83.label: text_chunk
196170eb-f8a8-4ac4-9896-ce4e9ddcfd83.text: Let A be the alphabet of a language, i.e., its set of characters, π a permutation on A , C a corpus and π ( C ) the corpus permuted by π . For example, if π ( a ) = e , then all “a” in C are replaced with “e” in π ( C ) . The learning procedure should learn identical equivalence classes on C and π ( C ) .

bf28baad-ddf8-4461-bb9e-a801321da93d.label: text_chunk
bf28baad-ddf8-4461-bb9e-a801321da93d.text: So, if g 1 ∼ τ g 2 after running the learning proce- dure on C , then π ( g 1 ) ∼ τ π ( g 2 ) after running the learning procedure on π ( C ) .This premise is motivated by our desire to come up with a general method that does not rely on spe- cific properties of a language or genre; e.g.

5a89b996-a37f-4205-8549-24cd8cf0dfaa.label: text_chunk
5a89b996-a37f-4205-8549-24cd8cf0dfaa.text: , the premise rules out exploiting the fact through fea- ture engineering that in many languages and gen- res, “c” and “C” are related. Such a relationship has to be learned from the data.

b5a50e55-9567-4a62-8b9f-730a21c30438.label: text_chunk
b5a50e55-9567-4a62-8b9f-730a21c30438.text:
3.3 Experiments

We run experiments on C , a 3 gigabyte English Wikipedia corpus, and train word2vec skipgram (W2V, (Mikolov et al., 2013)) and fastText skip- gram (FTX, (Bojanowski et al., 2017)) models on C and its derivatives. We randomly generate a permutation π on the alphabet and learn a trans- duction τ (details below).

f172a720-f90e-403f-bd92-8459d1db7401.label: text_chunk
f172a720-f90e-403f-bd92-8459d1db7401.text: In Table 2 (left), the columns “method”, π and τ indicate the method used (W2V or FTX) and whether experiments in a row were run on C , π ( C ) or τ ( π ( C )) . The val- ues of “whitespace” are: (i) ORIGINAL (white- space as in the original), (ii) SUBSTITUTE (what π outputs as whitespace is used as whitespace, i.e.

e2058ae7-99f4-466d-95ed-04e37837b8de.label: text_chunk
e2058ae7-99f4-466d-95ed-04e37837b8de.text: , π − 1 ( “ ” ) becomes the new whitespace) and (iii) RANDOM (random segmentation with parame- ters m = 50 , k min = 3 , k max = 9 ). Before random segmentation, whitespace is replaced with “@” – this character occurs rarely in C , so that the ef- fect of conflating two characters (original “@” and whitespace) can be neglected.

8a4cc251-22c9-478b-98ef-10f8510e5083.label: text_chunk
8a4cc251-22c9-478b-98ef-10f8510e5083.text: The random seg- menter then indicates boundaries by whitespace – unambiguously since it is applied to text that con- tains no whitespace.We learn τ on the embeddings learned by W2V on the random segmentation version of π ( C ) (C-RANDOM in the table) as described in § 3.2

9f173602-cabb-4bd8-aa74-0223648b4d7e.label: text_chunk
9f173602-cabb-4bd8-aa74-0223648b4d7e.text: 3 An example of such an inadvertant use of text prepro- cessing heuristics is that fastText seems to default to low- ercase ngrams if embeddings of uppercase ngrams are not available: when fastText is trained on lowercased text and then applied to uppercased text, it still produces embeddings.Table 1: String operations that on average do not change meaning. “@” stands for space.

93bfd9eb-c471-4eff-895c-589ecb530353.label: text_chunk
93bfd9eb-c471-4eff-895c-589ecb530353.text:
‡ is the left or right boundary of the ngram.

for N o = 200 .

58c2dbf2-8150-433b-b6a0-e22f694df446.label: text_chunk
58c2dbf2-8150-433b-b6a0-e22f694df446.text: Since the number of equiva- lence classes is much smaller than the number of ngrams, τ reduces the number of distinct char- acter ngrams from 758M in the random segmen- tation version of π ( C ) (C/D-RANDOM) to 96M in the random segmentation version of τ ( π ( C )) (E/F-RANDOM).Table 1 shows a selection of the N o operations.

ce8480bd-a4cb-45f6-9bed-228cb1e9a34e.label: text_chunk
ce8480bd-a4cb-45f6-9bed-228cb1e9a34e.text: Throughout the paper, if we give examples from π ( C ) or τ ( π ( C )) as we do here, we convert char- acters back to the original for better readability. The two uppercase/lowercase conversions shown in the table (E → e, C → c) were the only ones that were learned (we had hoped for more).

c366027b-3f2a-4dd6-9b0d-49be5bd95406.label: text_chunk
c366027b-3f2a-4dd6-9b0d-49be5bd95406.text: The post- deletion rule ml → m usefully rewrites “html” as “htm”, but is likely to do more harm than good. We inspected all 200 rules and, with a few excep- tions like ml → m, they looked good to us.Evaluation.

8eb9bfe0-8d2e-4dd2-96b9-cb93fdd2fee5.label: text_chunk
8eb9bfe0-8d2e-4dd2-96b9-cb93fdd2fee5.text: We evaluate the three models on an entity typing task, similar to (Yaghoobzadeh and Sch¨utze, 2015), but based on an entity dataset released by Xie et al. (2016) in which each en- tity has been assigned one or more types from a set of 50 types.

c417e4a0-61f5-4800-93c0-aacfaaecde90.label: text_chunk
c417e4a0-61f5-4800-93c0-aacfaaecde90.text: For example, the entity “Harri- son Ford” has the types “actor”, “celebrity” and “award winner” among others. We extract men- tions from FACC ( http://lemurproject. org/clueweb12/FACC1 ) if an entity has a mention there or we use the Freebase name as the mention otherwise.

cdd0414f-3051-4192-98ca-33c7e13f6665.label: text_chunk
cdd0414f-3051-4192-98ca-33c7e13f6665.text: This gives us a data set of 54,334, 6085 and 6747 mentions in train, dev and test, respectively. Each mention is annotated with the types that its entity has been assigned by Xie et al. (2016). The evaluation has a strong cross-domain aspect because of differences be- tween FACC and Wikipedia, the training corpus for our representations.

7f106d95-2748-457f-8a1a-ef2b83bb9856.label: text_chunk
7f106d95-2748-457f-8a1a-ef2b83bb9856.text:
For example, of the 525 mentions in dev that have a length of at least 5 and do not contain lowercase characters, more than half have 0 or 1 occurrences in the Wikipedia cor-
pus, including many like “JOHNNY CARSON” that are frequent in other case variants.

d25389b7-a48e-41a4-9881-251049d431e5.label: text_chunk
d25389b7-a48e-41a4-9881-251049d431e5.text: Since our goal in this experiment is to eval- uate tokenization-free learning, not tokenization- free utilization, we use a simple utilization base- line, the bag-of-ngram model (see § 1). A mention is represented as the sum of all character ngrams that embeddings were learned for.

16129640-3b2a-42db-8df3-bd87c27726b4.label: text_chunk
16129640-3b2a-42db-8df3-bd87c27726b4.text:
Linear SVMs (Chang and Lin, 2011) are then trained, one for each of the 50 types, on train and applied to dev and test.

 Our evaluation measure is micro F 1 on all typing decisions; e.g., one typing decision is: “Harrison Ford” is a mention of type “actor”. We tune thresholds on dev to optimize F 1 and then use these thresholds on test.

685ce808-371a-4c7f-a67c-edf60455c1aa.label: text_chunk
685ce808-371a-4c7f-a67c-edf60455c1aa.text:
3.4 Results

Results are presented in Table 2 (left). Overall performance of FTX is higher than W2V in all cases. For ORIGINAL, FTX’s recall is a lot higher than W2V’s whereas precision decreases slightly. This indicates that FTX is stronger in both learn- ing and application: in learning it can generalize better from sparse training data and in application it can produce representations for OOVs and better representations for rare words.

45b38bce-86db-4208-826a-2a008998af95.label: text_chunk
45b38bce-86db-4208-826a-2a008998af95.text: For English, pre- fixes, suffixes and stems are of particular impor- tance, but there often is not a neat correspondence between these traditional linguistic concepts and internal FTX representations; e.g., Bojanowski et al. (2017) show that “asphal”, “sphalt” and “phalt” are informative character ngrams of “asphaltic”.

eda84092-5413-436e-93da-0c0babceebad.label: text_chunk
eda84092-5413-436e-93da-0c0babceebad.text: Running W2V on random segmentations can be viewed as an alternative to the learning mecha- nism of FTX, which is based on character ngram cooccurrence; so it is not surprising that for RAN- DOM, FTX has only a small advantage over W2V.For C/D-SUBSTITUTE, we see a dramatic loss in performance if tokenization heuristics are not used.

404edcf6-ef8a-4922-9ad1-8e60429cd00b.label: text_chunk
404edcf6-ef8a-4922-9ad1-8e60429cd00b.text: This is not surprising, but shows how pow- erful tokenization can be.C/D-ORIGINAL is like C/D-SUBSTITUTE ex- cept that we artificially restored the space – so the permutation π is applied to all characters ex- cept for space. By comparing C/D-ORIGINAL and C/D-SUBSTITUTE, we see that the space is the most important text preprocessing feature em- ployed by W2V and FTX.

486352ac-bd7b-4d86-b6a5-d99ad6b5408a.label: text_chunk
486352ac-bd7b-4d86-b6a5-d99ad6b5408a.text:
If space is restored, there is only a small loss of performance compared to A/B-ORIGINAL. So text preprocessing heuris- tics other than whitespace tokenization in a nar-
whitespace ORIGINAL SUBSTITUTE RANDOM measure P R F 1 P R F 1 P R F 1 method π τ A W2V − − .538 .566 .552 .525 .596 .558 B FTX − − .530 .628 .575 .528 .608 .

af2979ea-1011-412f-98bf-e27f249a9082.text: 582 query neighbor r 1 Abdulaziz Abdul Azi 2 2 codenamed code name 1 3 Quarterfi uarter-Fi 1 4 worldreco orld-reco 1 5 antibodie stem cell 1 6 eflectors ear wheel 1 7 ommandeer rash land 1 8 reenplays ripts for 1 9 roughfare ugh downt 1 10 ilitating e-to-face 1

74222ad5-7b21-4078-8e30-5f00ae7e9071.label: text_chunk
74222ad5-7b21-4078-8e30-5f00ae7e9071.text: Table 2: Left: Evaluation results for named entity typing. Right: Neighbors of character ngrams. Rank r = 1 / r = 2 : nearest / second-nearest neighbor.row definition of the term (e.g., downcasing) do not seem to play a big role, at least not for our en- tity typing task.

ecd9f90f-02e3-4110-8334-41fe034654fc.label: text_chunk
ecd9f90f-02e3-4110-8334-41fe034654fc.text: For tokenization-free embedding learning on random segmentation, there is almost no differ- ence between original data (A/B-RANDOM) and permuted data (C/D-RANDOM). This confirms that our proposed learning method is insensitive to permutations and makes no use of text prepro- cessing heuristics.We achieve an additional improvement by ap- plying the transduction τ . In fact, FTX perfor- mance for F-RANDOM ( F 1 of .

66d42042-9e97-4ee4-84ff-9ba6453715fa.label: text_chunk
66d42042-9e97-4ee4-84ff-9ba6453715fa.text:
582) is better than tokenization-based W2V and FTX performance. Thus, our proposed method seems to be an effec- tive tokenization-free alternative to tokenization- based embedding learning.

3.5 Analysis of ngram embeddings

Table 2 (right) shows nearest neighbors of ten character ngrams, for the A-RANDOM space. Queries were chosen to contain only alphanumeric characters.

718afabd-76cb-4a54-ad65-3107862f04ec.label: text_chunk
718afabd-76cb-4a54-ad65-3107862f04ec.text: To highlight the difference to symbol- based representation models, we restricted the search to 9-grams that contained a delimiter at po- sitions 3, 4, 5, 6 or 7.Lines 1–4 show that “delimiter variation”, i.e.

e412143c-d80a-4243-946e-1f9353a2e67b.label: text_chunk
e412143c-d80a-4243-946e-1f9353a2e67b.text: , cases where a word has two forms, one with a de- limiter, one without a delimiter, is handled well: “Abdulaziz” / “Abdul Azi”, “codenamed” / “code name”, “Quarterfinal” / “Quarter-Final”, “world- record” / “world-record”.

c9940d1b-54b9-48c4-8220-046999656b70.label: text_chunk
c9940d1b-54b9-48c4-8220-046999656b70.text: Lines 5–9 are cases of ambiguous or polyse- mous words that are disambiguated through “char- acter context”. “stem”, “cell”, “rear”, “wheel”, “crash”, “land”, “scripts”, “through”, “downtown” all have several meanings.

10a0fd83-5c65-4dc3-bb15-d5383a12fbf1.label: text_chunk
10a0fd83-5c65-4dc3-bb15-d5383a12fbf1.text:
In contrast, the mean- ings of “stem cell”, “rear wheel”, “crash land”, “(write) scripts for” and “through downtown” are less ambiguous. A multiword recognizer may find the phrases “stem cell” and “crash land” auto-
matically.

e202659e-813e-4e90-9cf4-fa27333fcf9f.label: text_chunk
e202659e-813e-4e90-9cf4-fa27333fcf9f.text: But the examples of “scripts for” and “through downtown” show that what is accom- plished here is not multiword detection, but a more general use of character context for disambigua- tion.Line 10 shows that a 9-gram of “face-to-face” is the closest neighbor to a 9-gram of “facilitating”. This demonstrates that form and meaning some- times interact in surprising ways.

ea337573-8467-4b94-aa67-b2c533542d0e.label: text_chunk
ea337573-8467-4b94-aa67-b2c533542d0e.text:
Facilitating a meeting is most commonly done face-to-face.

 It is not inconceivable that form – the shared trigram “fac” or the shared fourgram “faci” in “facilitate” / “facing” – is influencing meaning here in a way that also occurs historically in cases like “ear” ‘or- gan of hearing’ / “ear” ‘head of cereal plant’, orig- inally unrelated words that many English speakers today intuit as one word.

ef14dc5e-eea0-4098-96ce-b9b179319cd5.label: text_chunk
ef14dc5e-eea0-4098-96ce-b9b179319cd5.text:
4 Utilization: Tokenization-free representation of text

4.1 Methodology

The main text representation model that is based on ngram embeddings similar to ours is the bag- of-ngram model . A sequence of characters is rep- resented by a single vector that is computed as the sum of the embeddings of all ngrams that occur in the sequence. In fact, this is what we did in the entity typing experiment.

6f0f9d35-3646-4029-afe8-067ee4a26834.label: text_chunk
6f0f9d35-3646-4029-afe8-067ee4a26834.text: In most work on bag-of-ngram models, the sequences considered are words or phrases.

839ca965-4d6f-4b2b-8f22-7c0f97517d42.label: text_chunk
839ca965-4d6f-4b2b-8f22-7c0f97517d42.text: In a few cases, the model is applied to longer sequences, including sentences and documents; e.g., (Sch¨utze, 1992), (Wieting et al., 2016).The basic assumption of the bag-of-ngram model is that sequence information is encoded in the character ngrams and therefore a “bag-of” ap- proach (which usually throws away all sequence information) is sufficient.

127650fd-35a5-47de-bdfb-702a1d4d68cf.label: text_chunk
127650fd-35a5-47de-bdfb-702a1d4d68cf.text:
The assumption is not implausible: for most bags of character sequences, there is only a single way of stitching them to-
gether to one coherent sequence, so in that case information is not necessarily lost (although this is likely when embeddings are added). But the as- sumption has not been tested experimentally.Here, we propose position embeddings , character-ngram-based embeddings that more fully preserve sequence information.

4db2966f-a0c3-4f18-a57c-aa74cd4b1903.label: text_chunk
4db2966f-a0c3-4f18-a57c-aa74cd4b1903.text: 4 The simple idea is to represent each position as the sum of all ngrams that contain that position. When we set k min = 3 , k max = 9 , this means that the position is the sum of ( P 3 ≤ k ≤ 9 k ) ngram embeddings (if all of these ngrams have embeddings, which gen- erally will be true for some, but not for most po- sitions).

0ee67bec-2231-4b5f-bb0b-b37bf47206e8.label: text_chunk
0ee67bec-2231-4b5f-bb0b-b37bf47206e8.text:
A sequence of n characters is then rep- resented as a sequence of n such position embed- dings.

4.2 Experiments

We again use the embeddings corresponding to A-RANDOM in Table 2. We randomly selected 2,000,000 contexts of size 40 characters from Wikipedia.

ecd2585d-4891-4013-860c-c65793b87cee.label: text_chunk
ecd2585d-4891-4013-860c-c65793b87cee.text:
We then created a noise context for each of the 2,000,000 contexts by replacing one character at position i ( 15 ≤ i ≤ 25 , uniformly sampled) with space (probability p = . 5 ) or a random character otherwise.

 Finally, we selected 1000 noise contexts randomly and computed their nearest neighbors among the 4,000,000 contexts (excluding the noise query).

901821a8-c82f-46f5-843c-a06274714daf.label: text_chunk
901821a8-c82f-46f5-843c-a06274714daf.text:
We did this in two different conditions: for a bag-of-ngram represen- tation of the context (sum of all character ngrams) and for the concatenation of 11 position embed- dings, those between 15 and 25.

 Our evaluation measure is mean reciprocal rank of the clean con- text corresponding to the noise context.

1303ad06-cb7c-44a0-ae4f-b816c4a02539.label: text_chunk
1303ad06-cb7c-44a0-ae4f-b816c4a02539.text: This simu- lates a text denoising experiment: if the clean con- text has rank 1, then the noisy context can be cor- rected.Table 4 shows that sequence-preserving po- sition embeddings perform better than bag-of- ngram representations.

25045a27-84a2-4abd-a7e5-4b4eb76eba44.label: text_chunk
25045a27-84a2-4abd-a7e5-4b4eb76eba44.text:
Table 5 shows an example of a context in which position embeddings did better than bag- of-ngrams, demonstrating that sequence informa- tion is lost by bag-of-ngram representations, in this case the exact position of “Seahawks”.Table 3 gives further intuition about the type of information position embeddings contain, show- ing the ngram embeddings closest to selected posi-
tion embeddings; e.g.

fe8296a7-c638-462b-a71c-5e81386e108f.label: text_chunk
fe8296a7-c638-462b-a71c-5e81386e108f.text:
, “estseller” (the first 9-gram on the line numbered 3 in the table) is closest to the embedding of position 3 (corresponding to the first “s” of “best-selling”). The kNN search space is restricted to alphanumeric ngrams.

5 Discussion

Single vs. multiple segmentation. The motiva- tion for multiple segmentation is exhaustive cov- erage of the space of possible segmentations .

89ec5416-fdd8-48fd-ba9b-22629d5b5a31.label: text_chunk
89ec5416-fdd8-48fd-ba9b-22629d5b5a31.text: An alternative approach would be to attempt to find a single optimal segmentation .Our intuition is that in many cases overlap- ping segments contain complementary informa- tion . Table 6 gives an example. Historic exchange rates are different from floating exchange rates and this is captured by the low similarity of the ngrams ic@exchang and ing@exchan .

7d2aa832-c528-4348-8ff5-4becda9c83fe.label: text_chunk
7d2aa832-c528-4348-8ff5-4becda9c83fe.text: Also, the meaning of “historic” and “floating” is non- compositional: these two words take on a special- ized meaning in the context of exchange rates. The same is true for “rates”: its meaning is not its gen- eral meaning in the compound “exchange rates”.

d6e9765c-6ba0-46a6-a062-bd9200e182da.label: text_chunk
d6e9765c-6ba0-46a6-a062-bd9200e182da.text: Thus, we need a representation that contains over- lapping segments, so that “historic” / “floating” and “exchange” can disambiguate each other in the first part of the compound and “exchange” and “rates” can disambiguate each other in the second part of the compound. A single segmentation can- not capture these overlapping ngrams.What text-type are tokenization-free ap- proaches most promising for?

c33b0ab4-96bb-4d49-baf3-ae7ab6eeb82d.label: text_chunk
c33b0ab4-96bb-4d49-baf3-ae7ab6eeb82d.text: The reviewers thought that language and text-type were badly chosen for this paper. Indeed, a morphologically complex language like Turkish and a noisy text- type like Twitter would seem to be better choices for a paper on robust text representation.However, robust word representation methods like FTX are effective for within-token general- ization, in particular, effective for both complex morphology and OOVs.

f5fa8b25-e8e7-49e5-a15c-235d7478f215.label: text_chunk
f5fa8b25-e8e7-49e5-a15c-235d7478f215.text: If linguistic variability and noise only occur on the token level, then a tokenization-free approach has fewer advantages.

7772acfa-fcf5-47fb-b14d-41ba71cbadb8.label: text_chunk
7772acfa-fcf5-47fb-b14d-41ba71cbadb8.text: On the other hand, the foregoing discussion of cross-token regularities and disambiguation ap- plies to well-edited English text as much as it does to other languages and other text-types as the example of “exchange” shows (which is dis- ambiguated by prior context and provides disam- biguating context to following words) and as is also exemplified by lines 5–9 in Table 2 (right).

fd566232-aeb8-4218-96b7-a166657cb168.label: text_chunk
fd566232-aeb8-4218-96b7-a166657cb168.text:
4 Position embeddings were independently proposed by Kalchbrenner et al. (2016), see Section 3.6 of their paper.

a44d1128-4651-41a9-8884-8acf67b09a01.label: text_chunk
a44d1128-4651-41a9-8884-8acf67b09a01.text: Table 3: Nearest ngram embeddings (rank r ∈ [1 , 5] ) of the position embeddings for “POS”, the posi- tions 2/3 (best), 15/16 (monthly), 23/24 (comic), 29/30 (book) and 34/35 (publications) in the Wikipedia excerpt “best-selling monthly comic book publications sold in North America”

d2b3741b-4476-4724-90ee-772a0cafc0a3.label: text_chunk
d2b3741b-4476-4724-90ee-772a0cafc0a3.text: Still, this paper does not directly evaluate the different contributions that within-token charac- ter ngram embeddings vs. cross-token character ngram embeddings make, so this is an open ques- tion. One difficulty is that few corpora are avail- able that allow the separate evaluation of white- space tokenization errors; e.g., OCR corpora gen- erally do not distinguish a separate class of white- space tokenization errors.Position embeddings vs.

747411f4-985f-4354-8601-dbedaf908b76.label: text_chunk
747411f4-985f-4354-8601-dbedaf908b76.text: phrase/sentence em- beddings. Position embeddings may seem to stand in opposition to phrase/sentence embed- dings. For many tasks, we need a fixed length rep- resentation of a longer sequence; e.g., sentiment analysis models compute a fixed-length represen- tation to classify a sentence as positive / negative.

0b4e8c0d-6b47-493e-ac2a-3e1c490f21cf.label: text_chunk
0b4e8c0d-6b47-493e-ac2a-3e1c490f21cf.text: To see that position embeddings are compatible with fixed-length embeddings, observe first that, in principle, there is no difference between word embeddings and position embeddings in this re- spect. Take a sequence that consists of, say, 6 words and 29 characters. The initial representation of the sentence has length 6 for word embeddings and length 29 for position embeddings.

9ab37e99-3fab-48af-93e1-b52620976ccb.label: text_chunk
9ab37e99-3fab-48af-93e1-b52620976ccb.text: In both cases, we need a model that reduces the variable length sequence into a fixed length vector at some intermediate stage and then classifies this vector as positive or negative. For example, both word and position embeddings can be used as the input to an LSTM whose final hidden unit activations are a fixed length vector of this type.bag-of-ngram position embeddings MRR .64 .76

d0ba7b4c-b1ca-420c-8764-f4287b4ad52c.label: text_chunk
d0ba7b4c-b1ca-420c-8764-f4287b4ad52c.text:
Table 4: Mean reciprocal rank of text denoising experiment for bag-of-ngram text representation and position embedding text representation
So assessing position embeddings is not a ques- tion of variable-length vs. fixed-length represen- tations. Word embeddings give rise to variable- length representations too. The question is solely whether the position-embedding representation is a more effective representation.

c04ddcc3-0898-484f-963d-b508a3a5381e.label: text_chunk
c04ddcc3-0898-484f-963d-b508a3a5381e.text: A more specific form of this argument con- cerns architectures that compute fixed-length rep- resentations of subsequences on intermediate lev- els, e.g., CNNs.

626ca51b-6781-4657-ab92-032ba32c9470.label: text_chunk
626ca51b-6781-4657-ab92-032ba32c9470.text: The difference between position- embedding-based CNNs and word-embedding- based CNNs is that the former have access to a vastly increased range of subsequences , includ- ing substrings of words (making it easier to learn that “exchange” and “exchanges” are related) and cross-token character strings (making it easier to learn that “exchange rate” is noncompositional).

806efb93-ab7d-49b2-a66d-67e5600837a1.label: text_chunk
806efb93-ab7d-49b2-a66d-67e5600837a1.text: Here, the questions are: (i) how useful are sub- sequences made available by position embeddings and (ii) is the increased level of noise and de- creased efficiency caused by many useless subse- quences worth the information gained by adding useful subsequences.Independence of training and utilization. We note that our proposed training and utiliza- tion methods are completely independent.

e487664b-a952-46b3-8c8f-81844cf3c0a2.label: text_chunk
e487664b-a952-46b3-8c8f-81844cf3c0a2.text: Posi- tion embeddings can be computed from any set of character-ngram-embeddings (including FTX) and our character ngram learning algorithm could be used for applications other than position em- beddings, e.g., for computing word embeddings.Context-free vs. context-sensitive embed- dings. Word embeddings are context-free: a given word w like “king” is represented by the same em- bedding independent of the context in which w occurs.

d1088c8f-dcca-49df-b909-6912f304f8b7.label: text_chunk
d1088c8f-dcca-49df-b909-6912f304f8b7.text:
Position embeddings are context-free as well: if the maximum size of a character ngram is k max , then the position embedding of the center of

f11ee721-b4c5-4820-8139-24a89f390bb5.label: text_chunk
f11ee721-b4c5-4820-8139-24a89f390bb5.text: Table 6: Cosine similarity of ngrams that cross word boundaries and disambiguate polysemous words. The tables show three disambiguating ngrams for “exchange” and “rates” that have dif- ferent meanings as indicated by low cosine sim- ilarity. In phrases like “floating exchange rates” and “historic exchange rates”, disambiguating ngrams overlap.

82ad24fb-9f37-4fbf-8743-7663eb68c054.label: text_chunk
82ad24fb-9f37-4fbf-8743-7663eb68c054.text: Parts of the word “exchange” are disambiguated by preceding context (ic@exchang, ing@exchan) and parts of “exchange” provide context for disambiguating “rates” (xchange@ra).a string s of length 2 k max − 1 is the same indepen- dent of the context in which s occurs.It is conceivable that text representations could be context-sensitive.

a2dd625b-f039-4bd9-bbc2-bf79525bf4ef.label: text_chunk
a2dd625b-f039-4bd9-bbc2-bf79525bf4ef.text: For example, the hidden states of a character language model have been used as a kind of nonsymbolic text representation (Chrupala, 2013; Evang et al., 2013; Chrupala, 2014) and these states are context-sensitive. How- ever, such models will in general be a second level of representation; e.g.

6134c07e-c68d-472a-a503-42989e72c08f.label: text_chunk
6134c07e-c68d-472a-a503-42989e72c08f.text: , the hidden states of a char- acter language model generally use character em- beddings as the first level of representation. Con- versely, position embeddings can also be the basis for a context-sensitive second-level text represen- tation. We have to start somewhere when we rep- resent text. Position embeddings are motivated by the desire to provide a representation that can be computed easily and quickly (i.e.

74d5a47f-cf25-4655-8251-a56ed6cb5f98.label: text_chunk
74d5a47f-cf25-4655-8251-a56ed6cb5f98.text:
, without taking context into account), but that on the other hand is
much richer than the symbolic alphabet.Processing text vs. speech vs. images. Gillick et al. (2016) write: “It is worth noting that noise is often added . . . to images . . . and speech where the added noise does not fundamentally alter the in- put, but rather blurs it.

be35343d-df85-4535-997c-6ff1c11ee558.label: text_chunk
be35343d-df85-4535-997c-6ff1c11ee558.text:
[bytes allow us to achieve] something like blurring with text.

” It is not clear to what extent blurring on the byte level is useful; e.g., if we blur the bytes of the word “university” individually, then it is unlikely that the noise gen- erated is helpful in, say, providing good training examples in parts of the space that would other- wise be unexplored.

d5f13784-0bcc-49a0-a590-2374e9faba2c.label: text_chunk
d5f13784-0bcc-49a0-a590-2374e9faba2c.text: In contrast, the text repre- sentation we have introduced in this paper can be blurred in a way that is analogous to images and speech. Each embedding of a position is a vector that can be smoothly changed in every direction. We have showed that the similarity in this space gives rise to natural variation.Prospects for completely tokenization-free processing.

c5e19a96-3c22-4668-b9c0-93c963abc25e.label: text_chunk
c5e19a96-3c22-4668-b9c0-93c963abc25e.text: We have focused on white- space tokenization and proposed a whitespace- tokenization-free method that computes embed- dings of higher quality than tokenization-based methods. However, there are many properties of edited text beyond whitespace tokenization that a complex rule-based tokenizer exploits. In a small explorative experiment, we replaced all non- alphanumeric characters with whitespace and re- peated experiment A-ORIGINAL for this setting. This results in an F 1 of .593, better by .

6603d665-f05d-4a99-a3c5-9a2323db9067.label: text_chunk
6603d665-f05d-4a99-a3c5-9a2323db9067.text:
01 than the best tokenization-free method. This illustrates that there is still a lot of work to be done before we can obviate the need for tokenization.

6 Related work5

In the following, we will present an overview of work on character-based models for a variety of tasks from different NLP areas. 6
The history of character-based research in NLP is long and spans a broad array of tasks.

69191063-51ce-47b9-8801-d8c763303a8b.label: text_chunk
69191063-51ce-47b9-8801-d8c763303a8b.text:
Here we make an attempt to categorize the literature of character-level work into three classes based on the way they incorporate character-level in- formation into their computational models.

 The three classes we identified are: tokenization- based models , bag-of-n-gram models and end- to-end models . However, there are also mix- tures possible, such as tokenization-based bag-of- n-gram models or bag-of-n-gram models trained end-to-end.

e221d582-b928-407d-bb36-3a6acf4e0f90.label: text_chunk
e221d582-b928-407d-bb36-3a6acf4e0f90.text:
On top of the categorization based on the under- lying representation model, we sub-categorize the work within each group into six abstract types of NLP tasks (if possible) to be able to compare them more directly. These task types are the following:

1. Representation learning for character se- quences:

 Work in this category attempts to learn a generic representation for sequences of characters in an unsupervised fashion on large corpora.

0c56b53d-7f0c-4911-989e-5515652c2a7c.label: text_chunk
0c56b53d-7f0c-4911-989e-5515652c2a7c.text:
Learning such representa- tions has been shown to be useful for solv- ing downstream NLP tasks (Collobert et al., 2011; Kocmi and Bojar, 2016; Mikolov et al., 2013).

2. Sequence-to-sequence generation:

 This category includes a variety of NLP tasks mapping variable-length input sequences to variable-length output sequences.

6dbb94fd-ee1a-4574-bd9a-ee3487e370ad.label: text_chunk
6dbb94fd-ee1a-4574-bd9a-ee3487e370ad.text: Tasks in this category include those that are naturally suited for character-based modeling, such as grapheme-to-phoneme conversion (Bisani and Ney, 2008; Kaplan and Kay, 1994; Sejnowski and Rosenberg, 1987), translit- eration (Haizhou et al.

c4253b48-a815-40ad-b6c2-d3435e53db7f.label: text_chunk
c4253b48-a815-40ad-b6c2-d3435e53db7f.text:
, 2004; Knight and Graehl, 1998; Sajjad, 2012), spelling nor- malization for historical text (Pettersson et al., 2014), or diacritics restauration (Mihal- cea and Nastase, 2002). Machine translation
and question answering are other major ex- amples of this category.

3.

d912480f-4d68-4521-8faf-ae3f493c6a99.label: text_chunk
d912480f-4d68-4521-8faf-ae3f493c6a99.text:
Sequence labeling:

 NLP tasks that assign a categorical label to a part of a sequence (a character, a sequence of characters or a to- ken) are included within this group. Part- of-speech tagging, named entity recognition, morphological segmentation and word align- ment are exemplary instances of sequence la- beling.

4. Language modeling:

 The other type of task for that character-based modeling has been important for a very long time is lan- guage modeling.

657a796f-aaf3-4b73-abeb-1851e6681cc4.label: text_chunk
657a796f-aaf3-4b73-abeb-1851e6681cc4.text:
In 1951, Shannon (Shan- non, 1951) proposed a guessing game ask- ing “How well can the next letter of a text be predicted when the preceding N letters are known?” This is basically the task of character-based language modeling.

5. Information retrieval:

 The information re- trieval task is to retrieve the most relevant character sequence to a given character se- quence (the query) from a set of existing character sequences.

6.

3230b105-ce86-41d6-bec1-25ecb2100678.label: text_chunk
3230b105-ce86-41d6-bec1-25ecb2100678.text:
Sequence classification: In this type of NLP task,

 a categorical label will be assigned to a character sequence (e.g., a document). In- stances of this type are language identifica- tion, sentiment classification, authorship at- tribution, topic classification and word sense disambiguation.

6.1 Tokenization-based Approaches

We group character-level models that are based on tokenization as a necessary preprocessing step in the category of tokenization-based approaches.

16c0c7cc-5b1b-4970-a088-5ccf0f30849c.label: text_chunk
16c0c7cc-5b1b-4970-a088-5ccf0f30849c.text:
Those can be either models with tokenized text as input or models that operate only on individual to- kens (such as studies on morphological inflection of words).

In the following paragraphs, we cover a subset of tokenization-based models that are used for rep- resentation learning, sequence-to-sequence gener- ation, sequence labeling, language modeling, and sequence classification tasks.Representation learning for character se- quences.

cc368a5d-6cb3-4123-b9b5-d53cd9c4f8d8.label: text_chunk
cc368a5d-6cb3-4123-b9b5-d53cd9c4f8d8.text:
Creating word representations based on characters has attracted much attention recently.5 This section was written in September 2016 and revised in April 2017.

 To suggest corrections and additional refer- ences, please send mail to inquiries@cislmu.org
6 In our view, morpheme-based models are not true in- stances of character-level models as linguistically motivated morphological segmentation is an equivalent step to tokeniza- tion, but on a different level.

bedd4c7e-ac20-4e15-9b7e-561fabba32aa.label: text_chunk
bedd4c7e-ac20-4e15-9b7e-561fabba32aa.text:
We therefore do not cover most work on morphological segmentation in this paper.

Such representations can model rare words, com- plex words, out-of-vocabulary words and noisy texts. In comparison to traditional word represen- tation models that learn separate vectors for word types, character-level models are more compact as they only need vector representations for charac- ters as well as a compositional model.Various neural network architectures have been proposed for learning token representations based on characters.

523c72b2-8186-4f51-a25b-245eaad7ebd6.label: text_chunk
523c72b2-8186-4f51-a25b-245eaad7ebd6.text: Examples of such architectures are averaging character embeddings, (bidirectional) recurrent neural networks (RNNs) (with or with- out gates) over character embeddings and convolu- tional neural networks (CNNs) over character em- beddings. Studies on the general task of learn- ing word representations from characters include (Chen et al., 2015; Ling et al., 2015b; Luong et al.

fb8f8520-c4c5-4267-94da-f86af75902a5.label: text_chunk
fb8f8520-c4c5-4267-94da-f86af75902a5.text: , 2013; Vylomova et al., 2016). These character- based word representations are often combined with word embeddings and integrated into a hi- erarchical system, such as hierarchical RNNs or CNNs or combinations of both to solve other task types. We will provide more concrete examples in the following paragraphs.Sequence-to-sequence generation (machine translation). Character-based machine translation is no new topic.

dc781265-36b1-41a1-9d15-5ed4b08acb53.label: text_chunk
dc781265-36b1-41a1-9d15-5ed4b08acb53.text: Using character-based methods has been a natural way to overcome challenges like rare words or out-of-vocabulary words in ma- chine translation. Traditional machine translation models based on characters or character n-grams have been investigated by (Lepage and Denoual, 2005; Tiedemann and Nakov, 2013; Vilar et al., 2007).

46980ec6-8cd9-41bd-8394-b70b95788535.label: text_chunk
46980ec6-8cd9-41bd-8394-b70b95788535.text: Neural machine translation with character- level and subword units has become popular re- cently (Costa-Juss`a and Fonollosa, 2016; Luong and Manning, 2016; Sennrich et al., 2016; Vylo- mova et al., 2016). In such neural models, using a joint attention/translation model makes joint learn- ing of alignment and translation possible (Ling et al.

f7656a8c-8f93-4567-b431-179d9563df2e.label: text_chunk
f7656a8c-8f93-4567-b431-179d9563df2e.text: , 2015b).Both hierarchical RNNs (Ling et al., 2015b; Luong and Manning, 2016) and combinations of CNNs and RNNs have been proposed for neural machine translation (Costa-Juss`a and Fonollosa, 2016; Vylomova et al., 2016).Sequence labeling.

559a49a9-16ff-48dc-9697-8b0dbd62983e.label: text_chunk
559a49a9-16ff-48dc-9697-8b0dbd62983e.text:
Examples of early efforts on sequence labeling using tokenization-based models include: bilingual character-level align- ment extraction (Church, 1993); unsupervised multilingual part-of-speech induction based on
characters (Clark, 2003); part-of-speech tagging with subword/character-level information (Andor et al.

b32f4640-5580-45be-9a2a-87d016250712.label: text_chunk
b32f4640-5580-45be-9a2a-87d016250712.text: , 2016; Hardmeier, 2016; Ratnaparkhi and others, 1996); morphological segmentation and tagging (Cotterell et al., 2016; M¨uller et al., 2013); and identification of language inclusion with character-based features (Alex, 2005).Recently, various hierarchical character-level neural networks have been applied to a variety of sequence labeling tasks.

0fa5fa43-0690-4e14-8290-4ed49ec69ace.label: text_chunk
0fa5fa43-0690-4e14-8290-4ed49ec69ace.text: • Recurrent neural networks are used for part- of-speech tagging (Ling et al.

902182db-43c9-4a18-a89c-8577bb52c6d9.label: text_chunk
902182db-43c9-4a18-a89c-8577bb52c6d9.text: , 2015a; Plank et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2016b), named entity recognition (Lample et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2016b), chunking (Yang et al., 2016b) and morphological segmentation/inflection gen- eration (Cao and Rei, 2016; Faruqui et al., 2016; Kann et al.

f56f8498-34f8-4f60-ba4d-5366f57a092e.label: text_chunk
f56f8498-34f8-4f60-ba4d-5366f57a092e.text: , 2016; Kann and Sch¨utze, 2016a; Kann and Sch¨utze, 2016b; Rastogi et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2016). Such hierarchical RNNs are also used for de- pendency parsing (Ballesteros et al., 2015).

cddea787-5b00-4677-92a1-f37fdcf8d343.label: text_chunk
cddea787-5b00-4677-92a1-f37fdcf8d343.text:
This work has shown that morphologically rich languages benefit from character-level models in dependency parsing.

• Convolutional neural networks are used for part-of-speech tagging (dos Santos and Zadrozny,

 2014) and named entity recogni- tion (dos Santos and Guimar˜aes, 2015).

• The combination of RNNs and CNNs is used, for instance,

 for named entity recognition.Language modeling.

7647245d-c5be-42df-82f0-e74fc5770f71.label: text_chunk
7647245d-c5be-42df-82f0-e74fc5770f71.text: Earlier work on sub-word language modeling has used morpheme-level fea- tures for language models (Bilmes and Kirch- hoff, 2003; Ircing et al., 2001; Kirchhoff et al., 2006; Shaik et al., 2013; Vergyri et al., 2004).

85dde8be-13a4-4194-880b-9f7e87c1b9d3.label: text_chunk
85dde8be-13a4-4194-880b-9f7e87c1b9d3.text: In addition, hybrid word/n-gram language models for out-of-vocabulary words have been applied to speech recognition (Hirsim¨aki et al., 2006; Kom- brink et al., 2010; Parada et al., 2011; Shaik et al., 2011).

bcf814ac-589f-46f4-8109-3fb5ceb44a1e.label: text_chunk
bcf814ac-589f-46f4-8109-3fb5ceb44a1e.text: Furthermore, characters and character n- grams have been used as input to restricted boltz- mann machine-based language models for ma- chine translation (Sperr et al., 2013).More recently, character-level neural language modeling has been proposed by a large body of work (Bojanowski et al., 2015; Botha and Blun- som, 2014; Kim et al., 2016; Ling et al.

3e9599d3-54ca-471c-9e2b-8899d98afec3.label: text_chunk
3e9599d3-54ca-471c-9e2b-8899d98afec3.text:
, 2015a; Mikolov et al., 2012; Shaik et al., 2013; Sperr
et al., 2013). Although most of this work is us- ing RNNs, there exist architectures that combine CNNs and RNNs (Kim et al., 2016). While most of these studies combine the output of the char- acter model with word embeddings, the authors of (Kim et al.

5988d119-a622-436e-bc5f-6a294e2f6dab.label: text_chunk
5988d119-a622-436e-bc5f-6a294e2f6dab.text: , 2016) report that this does not help them for their character-aware neural lan- guage model. They use convolution over character embeddings followed by a highway network (Sri- vastava et al., 2015) and feed its output into a long short-term memory network that predicts the next word using a softmax function.Sequence classification.

9ea5240f-351d-4a65-baba-a657bc2ef28c.label: text_chunk
9ea5240f-351d-4a65-baba-a657bc2ef28c.text:
Examples of tokenization-based models that perform sequence classification are CNNs used for sentiment classification (dos Santos and Gatti, 2014) and combinations of RNNs and CNNs used for language identification (Jaech et al., 2016).

6.2 Bag-of-n-gram Models

Character n-grams have a long history as fea- tures for specific NLP applications, such as in- formation retrieval.

bc5983ce-5f44-4ae7-a868-634876ae3b3c.label: text_chunk
bc5983ce-5f44-4ae7-a868-634876ae3b3c.text:
However, there is also work on representing words or larger input units, such as phrases, with character n-gram embeddings. Those embeddings can be within-token or cross- token, i.e., there is no tokenization necessary.

Although such models learn/use character n- gram embeddings from tokenized text or short text segments, to represent a piece of text, the occur- ring character n-grams are usually summed with- out the need for tokenization.

9129d9fd-079f-4f73-89fd-62fcbf68e830.label: text_chunk
9129d9fd-079f-4f73-89fd-62fcbf68e830.text: For example, the phrase “Berlin is located in Germany” is repre- sented with character 4-grams as follows: “Berl erli rlin lin in i n is is is l s lo loc loca ocat cate ated ted ed i d in in in G n Ge Ger Germ erma rman many any.” Note that the input has not been tokenized and there are n-grams spanning token boundaries.

1de9be76-11bd-4b4b-af1b-1472557c530b.label: text_chunk
1de9be76-11bd-4b4b-af1b-1472557c530b.text: We also include non-embedding ap- proaches using bag-of-n-grams within this group as they go beyond word and token representations.In the following, we explore a subset of bag- of-ngram models that are used for representation learning, information retrieval, and sequence clas- sification tasks.Representation learning for character se- quences. An early study in this category of character-based models is (Sch¨utze, 1992).

a97ecfb2-cb78-4d22-b806-d5ab9aab54c7.label: text_chunk
a97ecfb2-cb78-4d22-b806-d5ab9aab54c7.text:
Its goal is to create corpus-based fixed-length dis- tributed semantic representations for text. To train k-gram embeddings, the top character k-grams are
extracted from a corpus along with their cooccur- rence counts. Then, singular value decomposition (SVD) is used to create low dimensional k-gram embeddings given their cooccurrence matrix.

48f6f141-3172-4af9-b557-f8c49fe1e30b.label: text_chunk
48f6f141-3172-4af9-b557-f8c49fe1e30b.text: To apply them to a piece of text, the k-grams of the text are extracted and their corresponding embed- dings are summed. The study evaluates the k-gram embeddings in the context of word sense disam- biguation.A more recent study (Wieting et al., 2016) trains character n-gram embeddings in an end-to-end fashion with a neural network. They are evaluated on word similarity, sentence similarity and part- of-speech tagging.

d14efe40-6c5d-4eb0-a413-4044d24db479.label: text_chunk
d14efe40-6c5d-4eb0-a413-4044d24db479.text: Training character n-gram embeddings has also been proposed for biological sequences (Asgari and Mofrad, 2015; Asgari and Mofrad, 2016) for a variety of bioinformatics tasks.Information retrieval. As mentioned before, character n-gram features are widely used in the area of information retrieval (Cavnar, 1995; Chen et al.

8816ed66-3891-4b27-b9eb-ac90a02710c7.label: text_chunk
8816ed66-3891-4b27-b9eb-ac90a02710c7.text: , 1997; Damashek, 1995; De Heer, 1974; Mc- Namee and Mayfield, 2004; Kettunen et al., 2010).Sequence classification. Bag-of-n-gram mod- els are used for language identification (Baldwin and Lui, 2010; Dunning, 1994), topic labeling (Kou et al.

23a0f34e-d609-4110-b574-3c421b4032da.label: text_chunk
23a0f34e-d609-4110-b574-3c421b4032da.text:
, 2015), authorship attribution (Peng et al., 2003), word/text similarity (Bojanowski et al., 2017; Eyecioglu and Keller, 2016; Wieting et al., 2016) and word sense disambiguation (Sch¨utze, 1992).

6.3 End-to-end Models

Similar to bag-of-n-gram models, end-to-end models are tokenization-free.

fe4fe8e4-0c05-45c1-9fd3-5afd067b5230.label: text_chunk
fe4fe8e4-0c05-45c1-9fd3-5afd067b5230.text:
Their input is a se- quence of characters or bytes and they are directly optimized on a (task-specific) objective. Thus, they learn their own, task-specific representation of the input sequences. Recently, character-based end-to-end models have gained a lot of popularity due to the success of neural networks.

We explore the subset of these models that are used for sequence generation, sequence label- ing, language modeling and sequence classifica- tion tasks.Sequence-to-sequence generation.

a3918837-943c-474c-a54a-db882687a5b4.label: text_chunk
a3918837-943c-474c-a54a-db882687a5b4.text:
In 2011, the authors of (Sutskever et al., 2011) already pro- posed an end-to-end model for generating text. They train RNNs with multiplicative connections on the task of character-level language modeling. Afterwards, they use the model to generate text


and find that the model captures linguistic struc- ture and a large vocabulary.

8fdbd1b0-fe9f-48dd-ac05-96e6e16adcf4.label: text_chunk
8fdbd1b0-fe9f-48dd-ac05-96e6e16adcf4.text: It produces only a few uncapitalized non-words and is able to balance parantheses and quotes even over long distances (e.g., 30 characters). A similar study by (Graves, 2013) uses a long short-term memory network to create character sequences.

c38842c9-8b28-4e28-90a9-8dd8da799685.label: text_chunk
c38842c9-8b28-4e28-90a9-8dd8da799685.text: Recently, character-based neural network sequence-to-sequence models have been applied to instances of generation tasks like machine translation (Chung et al., 2016b; Kalchbrenner et al., 2016; Lee et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2016a) (which was previously proposed on the token-level (Sutskever et al.

2426cfdf-b2db-4512-b7fc-d4f5db0533b8.label: text_chunk
2426cfdf-b2db-4512-b7fc-d4f5db0533b8.text: , 2014)), question answering (Golub and He, 2016) and speech recognition (Bahdanau et al., 2016; Chan et al., 2016; Eyben et al., 2009; Graves and Jaitly, 2014).Sequence labeling.

8d72878a-14f4-4449-aa75-5eec8bb5fe5b.label: text_chunk
8d72878a-14f4-4449-aa75-5eec8bb5fe5b.text: Character and character n-gram-based features were already proposed in 2003 for named entity recognition in an end-to- end manner using a hidden markov model (Klein et al., 2003). More recently, the authors of (Ma and Hovy, 2016) have proposed an end-to-end neural network based model for named entity recognition and part-of-speech tagging.

ae51d262-e259-4d7a-8c23-d417d17c1327.label: text_chunk
ae51d262-e259-4d7a-8c23-d417d17c1327.text: An end- to-end model is also suggested for unsupervised, language-independent identification of phrases or words (Gerdjikov and Schulz, 2016).A prominent recent example of neural end-to- end sequence labeling is the paper by (Gillick et al., 2016) about multilingual language processing from bytes. A window is slid over the input se- quence, which is represented by its byte string.

967323fb-f36f-4733-97de-541c46684293.label: text_chunk
967323fb-f36f-4733-97de-541c46684293.text: Thus, the segments in the window can begin and end mid-word or even mid-character. The authors apply the same model for different languages and evaluate it on part-of-speech tagging and named entity recognition.Language modeling. The authors of (Chung et al., 2016a) propose a hierarchical multiscale recur- rent neural network for language modeling.

84479e1b-a6fc-468f-b7e8-8ef261c9eab6.label: text_chunk
84479e1b-a6fc-468f-b7e8-8ef261c9eab6.text: The model uses different timescales to encode tem- poral dependencies and is able to discover hier- archical structures in a character sequence with- out explicit tokenization. Other studies on end-to- end language models include (Kalchbrenner et al., 2016; Miyamoto and Cho, 2016).Sequence classification.

ef611f34-c209-46b4-8433-df28b06390b4.label: text_chunk
ef611f34-c209-46b4-8433-df28b06390b4.text:
Another recent end- to-end model uses character-level inputs for doc- ument classification (Xiao and Cho, 2016; Zhang and LeCun, 2015; Zhang et al., 2015). To capture
long-term dependencies of the input, the authors combine convolutional layers with recurrent lay- ers. The model is evaluated on sentiment analy- sis, ontology classification, question type classifi- cation and news categorization.

79a359dc-0271-4b62-88e1-f0184ade70f6.label: text_chunk
79a359dc-0271-4b62-88e1-f0184ade70f6.text:
End-to-end models are also used for entity typ- ing based on the character sequence of the entity’s name (Yaghoobzadeh and Sch¨utze, 2017).

7 Conclusion

We introduced the first generic text representa- tion model that is completely nonsymbolic, i.e., it does not require the availability of a segmen- tation or tokenization method that identifies words or other symbolic units in text.

c7fecf29-3e8b-4ea0-8e06-47a6e91c72c3.label: text_chunk
c7fecf29-3e8b-4ea0-8e06-47a6e91c72c3.text:
This is true for the training of the model as well as for apply- ing it when computing the representation of a new text.

 In contrast to prior work that has as- sumed that the sequence-of-character information captured by character ngrams is sufficient, posi- tion embeddings also capture sequence-of-ngram information. We showed that our model performs better than prior work on entity typing and text de- noising.

Future work.

2609d2e3-6291-4daf-883e-705bd429c7dd.label: text_chunk
2609d2e3-6291-4daf-883e-705bd429c7dd.text: The most important challenge that we need to address is how to use nonsymbolic text repre- sentation for tasks that are word-based like part- of-speech tagging. This may seem like a con- tradiction at first, but Gillick et al. (2016) have shown how character-based methods can be used for “symbolic” tasks. We are currently working on creating an analogous evaluation for our nonsym- bolic text representation.

8b9071cb-0cd9-480b-8c47-dd982495425f.label: text_chunk
8b9071cb-0cd9-480b-8c47-dd982495425f.text:

164e9083-4829-4c1b-a316-5c0b71962130.label: text_chunk
164e9083-4829-4c1b-a316-5c0b71962130.text:
8 Appendix

8.1 Sparseness in tokenization-free approaches

Nonsymbolic representation learning does not pre- process the training corpus by means of tokeniza- tion and considers many ngrams that would be ig- nored in tokenized approaches because they span token boundaries.

a4f4e530-1c25-49a2-b3f1-0cbcf57e326f.label: text_chunk
a4f4e530-1c25-49a2-b3f1-0cbcf57e326f.text:
As a result, the number of
Figure 1: The graph shows how many different character ngrams ( k min = 3 , k max = 10 ) oc- cur in the first n bytes of the English Wikipedia for symbolic (tokenization-based) vs.

 nonsym- bolic (tokenization-free) processing. The number of ngrams is an order of magnitude larger in the nonsymbolic approach.

bf656af3-7906-44a4-a40a-88a4e9a92dcf.label: text_chunk
bf656af3-7906-44a4-a40a-88a4e9a92dcf.text:
We counted all segments, corresponding to m = ∞ . For the experiments in the paper ( m = 50 ), the number of nonsymbolic character ngrams is smaller.ngrams that occur in a corpus is an order of mag- nitude larger for tokenization-free approaches than for tokenization-based approaches. See Figure 1.

8.3 Other hyperparameters

We did not tune N o = 200 , but results are highly sensitive to the value of this parameter. If N o is too small, then beneficial conflations (collapse punc- tuation marks, replace all digits with one symbol) are not found.

49f53f6f-d4a3-4ca6-9cbb-3d825b04d9f8.label: text_chunk
49f53f6f-d4a3-4ca6-9cbb-3d825b04d9f8.text:
If N o is too large, then precision
suffers – in the extreme case all characters are col- lapsed into one.

We also did not tune m = 50 , but we do not consider results to be very sensitive to the value of m if it is reasonably large.

e73b1565-c0a6-4529-9d75-e14ae4f318e9.label: text_chunk
e73b1565-c0a6-4529-9d75-e14ae4f318e9.text:
Of course, if a larger range of character ngram lengths is chosen, i.e., a larger interval [ k min , k max ] , then at some point m = 50 will not be sufficient and possible seg- mentations would not be covered well enough in sampling.

The type of segmentation used in multiple seg- mentation can also be viewed as a hyperparameter.

c0116a1c-9f63-46e2-a1f5-b5cda857b02d.label: text_chunk
c0116a1c-9f63-46e2-a1f5-b5cda857b02d.text: An alternative to random segmentation would be exhaustive segementation, but a naive implemen- tation of that strategy would increase the size of the training corpus by several orders of magnitude. Another alternative is to choose one fixed size, e.g., 4 or 5 (similar to (Sch¨utze, 1992)).

0a85784f-f8b5-4912-830d-1e0dc53fb125.label: text_chunk
0a85784f-f8b5-4912-830d-1e0dc53fb125.text:
Many of the nice disambiguation effects we see in Ta- ble 2 (right) and in Table 6 would not be possi- ble with short ngrams. On the other hand, a fixed ngram size that is larger, e.g., 10, would make it difficult to get 100% coverage: there would be po- sitions for which no position embedding can be computed.

community_summary.154:
### Summary of Relationships

1. **Symbolic Approach and Tokenizer**: The symbolic approach relies on the tokenizer to segment text into tokens, which is essential for its methodology.

2. **Text Representation Model and Training**: The Text Representation Model utilizes the training process to optimize its parameters based on the data it processes.

3. **RANDOM and Whitespace**: The RANDOM algorithm manipulates whitespace by replacing it with the '@' character during segmentation, establishing a direct relationship between the algorithm and the character type.

4. **fastText and ngrams**: fastText employs ngrams in its text processing and embedding generation techniques, which are vital for understanding and representing language structure.

5. **9-grams and Delimiter**: 9-grams use delimiters to define boundaries within sequences, facilitating structured analysis of text data.

6. **Sequence Classification and Tokenization-based Approaches**: Sequence classification depends on tokenization-based approaches as a preprocessing step to effectively categorize character sequences.

7. **Models and Tokenization**: Various models utilize tokenization as a fundamental process to convert text into manageable units (tokens) for further analysis.

8. **Averaging Character Embeddings and Character Embeddings**: Averaging character embeddings is a technique that uses character embeddings to create a single averaged representation.

9. **Neural Machine Translation and Joint Attention/Translation Model**: Neural Machine Translation employs the Joint Attention/Translation Model to enhance alignment and translation processes, making it a crucial element in translation workflows.

This summary encapsulates the key entities and their interrelationships, highlighting the methodologies and processes involved in text processing and analysis.

The relationships outlined involve two primary entities: **Position Embeddings** and **Character N-gram Embeddings**. 

1. **Position Embeddings** and **Character-ngram-based Embeddings** are both techniques utilized in natural language processing (NLP) aimed at improving text representation. Position embeddings emphasize the sequential order of words, while character-ngram-based embeddings concentrate on the structural aspects of text through character sequences.

2. **Position Embeddings** are derived from **Character N-gram Embeddings**, suggesting that the latter serves as a foundational method for the computation of position embeddings. This indicates a hierarchical relationship where character n-gram embeddings provide the basis for developing position embeddings.

In summary, the relationships highlight the interconnectedness of these NLP techniques, illustrating how character n-gram embeddings contribute to the formulation of position embeddings, thereby enhancing the overall representation of text in computational models.

The relationships outlined involve several key entities and their interactions in the context of text data evaluation:

This relationship highlights the importance of the Bag-of-Ngram method in assessing model performance.

2. **Corpora** and **Whitespace Tokenization Errors**: The evaluation of whitespace tokenization errors is a challenge, and corpora are utilized to assess these errors. This indicates that corpora play a crucial role in identifying and evaluating issues related to tokenization in text processing.

Overall, the summary emphasizes the significance of the Bag-of-Ngram Representation in model evaluation through Mean Reciprocal Rank, as well as the role of corpora in addressing whitespace tokenization errors. These relationships underscore the interconnectedness of text representation methods and evaluation techniques in natural language processing.

The relationships highlight the connection between "Gauging Similarity with N-grams" and "Language-independent Categorization." 

1. **Gauging Similarity with N-grams** is described as a method for assessing text similarity, which can be applied in a **Language-independent Categorization** context. This technique allows for the evaluation of text similarity without being constrained by the language in which the text is written.

2. Conversely, **Language-independent Categorization** is identified as a method that utilizes **Gauging Similarity with N-grams** to achieve its goal of categorizing text based on similarity, irrespective of language barriers.

In summary, the relationship emphasizes that Gauging Similarity with N-grams serves as a foundational technique for Language-independent Categorization, enabling effective text analysis across different languages.

### Summary of Relationships

1. **Pointwise Mutual Information Matrix & Skipgram Model**: The Skipgram Model utilizes the Pointwise Mutual Information Matrix to analyze word relationships based on their co-occurrence in various contexts. This highlights the model's reliance on statistical measures to enhance its understanding of language.

2. **A-RANDOM & Wikipedia**: A-RANDOM employs contexts selected from Wikipedia to generate its embeddings. This indicates a significant connection between the algorithm and Wikipedia as a data source, showcasing how external knowledge bases can inform machine learning processes.

3. **Nonsymbolic Representation Learning & Tokenization-free Approaches**: Nonsymbolic representation learning leverages tokenization-free approaches to process data, moving beyond traditional tokenization methods. This relationship emphasizes a more holistic understanding of data, allowing for richer representations without the limitations imposed by conventional tokenization.

Overall, these relationships illustrate the interconnectedness of various models and approaches in natural language processing, emphasizing the importance of data sources and innovative techniques in enhancing machine learning capabilities.

The relationships outlined in the knowledge graph highlight key connections between linguistic concepts and their applications in language processing:

1. **Ngram and Form-Meaning Homomorphism**: Ngrams are sequences of words that can be analyzed through the principle of form-meaning homomorphism, indicating that these sequences not only represent linguistic forms but also convey meaning, establishing a correspondence between structure and semantics.

2. **English Text and Disambiguation**: The clarity of well-edited English text relies on disambiguation, which is essential for ensuring that the meanings of words are clear and contextually appropriate, thereby enhancing comprehension and communication.

3. **Morphological Segmentation and Word Representation Models**: Morphological segmentation plays a crucial role in word representation models by providing a framework for breaking down words into smaller units. This decomposition allows for various representations of words, facilitating better understanding and processing in linguistic applications.

Overall, these relationships emphasize the interconnectedness of linguistic structures, the importance of clarity in language, and the methodologies used to represent and analyze language effectively.
